Adventure #4—No Use Crying Over Stolen Milk

Adventure #4—No Use Crying Over
Stolen Milk
Adventure #5—Lights... Camera... “All right,” I began as soon as I walked in the door the next day. I’d been up for hours the night before thinkingChange the Action
about the challenge.
Adventure #6—The Write Stuff
Adventure #7—Oh, What a Tangled “He’s not somehow siphoning off the milk on the way to the customer?”	Web We Weave Previous Lesson Next Lesson	Mark as Complete
Adventure #8—To Sleep, Perchance
to Come Up with Great Ideas Tom, who’d been reading the newspaper, didn’t seem surprised by my eagerness. “Nope, the detectives would
Adventure #9—The Beginning have seen him.”
Postscript... Four Years Later
“No hanky-panky at the customer sites?”
“No, the detectives would have seen that as well.”
“He’s not bribing the customers’ workers to steal it for him?”
“Nope, he’s on his own.”
I sighed, defeated. “Then, I give up.”
“Let me give you a hint. You’re not thinking backwards enough .”
“Well, that’s the first time I’ve ever been told off for not thinking backwards!”
“You see, often, we try to solve problems and come up with ideas that apply to only a limited time frame—such
as the time from when the driver arrives to have his truck filled up at the dairy farm. But this fellow was clever. He
must have known about the seventh box: Do It Beforehand .”
“Aha! So, he rigged up some way of stealing the milk before he arrived to pick it up.”
“Exactly. Can you figure out how?”
I thought for a while. “No,” I said finally. “How do you steal something before you even see it?”
“Tricky, eh? Here’s how. Before the driver set out for the dairy farm, he took a few large buckets and hung them
inside the truck. That way, when the dairy workers filled the truck with milk, the buckets got filled as well.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 8 --

“But when the tank was emptied at the processing plant, the buckets stayed full and the trucker was able to
reach in, pull it out, empty them at his leisure, and milk some profits by selling fresh milk to friends and family.
“This box, Do It Beforehand , encourages you to focus on what you can do earlier that will help you later. It’s
extraordinarily useful, because, as I said, people are often so focused on the problem itself—and what happens
when the problem occurs—that they don’t see what can be done before the issue rears its ugly head.
“Same goes for opportunities. People focus so much on what can be done during a certain window of time—the
time a machine is in operation or the time a customer is in the store—that they don’t take advantage of what
could be done before the machine does its job or before the customer walks in the door.”
Tom reached under his worktable and pulled out what looked like a piece of wood stained to a fiery red, clearly
switching gears yet again. “Like it?” he asked.
“Beautiful,” I replied, running my hands across the grain. “Nice, even stain job.”
“Nope—that wood is unstained .”
“Painted?” I ventured.
“Nope— un painted.”
“Wow. I’ve never seen trees naturally produce such a deep red color.”
“Neither have I, because there’s nothing natural about it. I’ll give you a hint—we ‘do it beforehand.’”
“You stain the wood beforehand?”
“Yup. I’m developing a system to send pigment to the roots, which then gets absorbed by the tree. Or, to put it a
little differently, we water the trees with tinted water.”
“Huh—I never would have thought to do that.”
“You would if you looked in this Do It Beforehand box. I’m also working with a medical supply company on a
better way to take casts off broken limbs once they’ve healed, without damaging the skin or scaring patients half
to death by chiseling or sawing away toward their skin. Got any ideas?”
“I’m guessing it won’t have anything to do with what’s done to the cast when it’s ready to come off, but rather
before it’s put on.”
“Exactly! I’ve found a way to embed a small saw blade into the cast. When it’s ready to come off, the doctor
hooks a handle to holes on each end of the blade and just starts cutting outward from within— away from the
skin.”
I shook my head in disbelief. This guy really was full of great ideas.
“Now, as for your bookstore… Pick a problem and let’s try to solve it inside this box.”
“Okay… ” I began slowly. “I’d like people to buy more books when they come in the store. But until now, I’ve only
looked at what I could do to sell them more after they walk in the door. What could I do before they come in?”
I pondered this carefully, looking around Tom’s workshop for inspiration. Just as I was about to give up, I noticed
a stack of books sitting on his desk. Boom—I had an idea.
“Hmmm… How about this? It seems like most people spend half their time in the store looking for books. What
if they could call or message before they come in, give us a list of the types of books or subject they might be
interested in, and we’d have a stack of books waiting for them when they arrive?”
“That’s a great idea,” said Tom. “I’d certainly want to do that myself instead of spending so much of my time
figuring out whether something I want is shelved under ‘physics,’ ‘science’ or something else entirely. And I’d
probably wind up buying more books than if I’d had to search for them myself.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 8 --

“I guess I really need to spend some time in this box,” I said. “Because it occurs to me that in both my marketing
and in my running the bookstore, I’ve been ignoring the ‘beforehand’ and focusing instead on what’s right at
hand. I need to take a good, hard look at things like planning and training my people before they start working
with customers.”
“You’re really starting to get the idea here,” said Tom. “And look, these boxes are just starting points. You never
know what you may find in them, and there’s no ‘right’ or ‘wrong.’ For example, another trick I use is to think of
boxes in terms of their opposites . So Do It Beforehand might make you think of things you don’t do—or need to
stop doing—beforehand.”
“I can see where I’m going to be able to solve a lot of my problems with these boxes,” I said. “But I’ve been
wanting to ask you… aren’t there just some things you can’t do anything about? Like, aren’t some things just
going to happen no matter what you do?”
“That’s a good point,” said Tom, seemingly glad I had brought it up. “And there’s a box for those tricksters as
well!”
“The trash box?”
“No, no. The Convert Into a Benefit box. It’s for things you can’t—or don’t want to—do anything about. Very
often, you can turn them into a benefit if you look at them a little more closely—or a little differently. You accept
that the problem or so-called ‘negative’ outcome is going to happen.
But, instead of seeing what you can do beforehand to prevent it, you see what you can do after to turn it into a
benefit! It’s the old lemons into lemonade trick—but it’s amazing how rarely most people actually employ this
technique.
“For example, we used to have a problem running acid through the pipes in a factory where I worked. It would
eat the heck out of the pipe’s interior in no time.
“Interestingly, we also discovered that when we pumped alkaline or base substances through the pipes, it left a
sediment. This was a problem as well, because the pipes would become clogged. So, we came up with the
clever solution of alternating between pumping acid and base liquids through the pipes—converting both
negatives into a positive.”
“Genius!” I exclaimed.
Tom barely even paused before launching into the next story. He had a way of shifting subjects quickly and
keeping you on your toes.
“And have you ever seen the sea lions at Fisherman’s Wharf in San Francisco?” he asked.
“Yes—it’s an amazing sight.” I recalled my trip to the wharf years before, where I’d witnessed hundreds of sea
lions sunning themselves, playing, fighting and making a racket. “And a great tourist attraction!”
“Right! But they weren’t always seen as such an asset. The story is that one day, a few seals started showing up
and sunning themselves on some floating docks next to Fisherman’s Wharf. Before long their friends and
relatives joined them and soon, there was a huge number of seals, and nothing that could be done about it.
There was no way to evict the seals from their new home.
All those seals got to be a real problem. They were loud. They were aggressive. They were messy. The boat
owners couldn’t get to their boats. And more and more were showing up every day.
“So, folks started coming up with all sorts of plans to get rid of the seals. Some proposed capturing them and
relocating them—very far away. Jacques Cousteau himself offered to rig up a diver in a shark fin to scare them
off. Some of the storeowners on the pier were probably ready to just shoot them.
But then, someone noticed that all the tourists actually loved watching the sea lions. That person then had the
idea to stop trying to deal with the ‘problem’ and just leave the sea lions alone—and turn them into a benefit. The
Wharf began promoting the sea lions as a reason to come to Fisherman’s Wharf. And that collection of tacky
seafood shacks and souvenir stands became more popular than ever.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 8 --

“And remember the driver who was stealing the milk?” Tom asked, suddenly shifting topics yet again.
“Sure,” I replied. “Are you going to tell me how to turn theft into a benefit?”
“In a way,” replied Tom. “The rest of the story is that instead of agonizing over all the money they had lost on
stolen milk, private detectives and unhappy customers…”
“And having to find a new driver,” I added.
“Yes, and instead of feeling sorry for themselves, they turned it into a benefit. Can you figure out how?”
“You said the driver was stealing it to sell to his family and friends. Maybe the dairy, instead of selling all their
milk to the big processor, should bottle some of it themselves and sell directly if there’s so much demand.”
“Right you are,” agreed Tom. “Sometimes what seems like a problem or negative is just a piece of machinery or
a system or—in business—a market trying to reveal an opportunity to us. They wound up setting up a nice little,
highly profitable side business bottling some of their milk themselves and selling it at a discount to their
employees and their friends and families.
“Now, let’s toss some of your problems or negative outcomes into this Turn It Into a Benefit box and see what
happens.”
“How about ‘returns?’” I suggested. “I hate it when people return books and want their money back. I know it’s
their right to do so according to our policy, but I can honestly just feel that money coming right out of my pocket.”
“Sometimes,” offered Tom, “you can turn a negative into a benefit by exaggerating it.”
“What do you mean?” I asked. “I should encourage people to return books?”
“Take sand and gravel,” Tom said, making another one of his unexpected shifts. “In very cold climates, sand or
gravel often stick together in transit and become difficult to unload. But, if you super freeze the mixture by adding
some liquid nitrogen, the sand or gravel goes from hard to brittle , breaks up and becomes a breeze to unload.
Just try doing the same with your ‘returns’ problem and see where it takes you.”
“All right, I’ll encourage them to return books,” I said, almost sarcastically at first, and then, before I knew it, the
ideas had started flowing. “Return all you want. In any condition. Take 30 days. Heck, take a couple of months.
Take a year, what do I care?” At this point, I felt like I was getting carried away, but the smile on Tom’s face
encouraged me to continue.
“Hey, you know, I could say all that. But very few people—if any—would really take advantage of it. And
meanwhile, it would certainly make people more comfortable about buying books in the first place. And it would
also make me stand out from the chain bookstores, with all their strict corporate policies of ‘no returns after 30
days’ and ‘books must be returned in original condition.’
“Whoa! I wonder if I can do this with all my store’s problems,” I blurted out, my head now swimming. “Let’s see…
negative: Too many teenagers hanging out in the store. Maybe we could try offering special events built around
books that teens would actually be interested in. Let’s turn them into customers .
“Hmmm… another negative might be: Not enough traffic in the store. Why should they have to come to the
store? Why not start a program that delivers books to people’s homes or businesses? I could hire an intern to
deliver books or contract with a delivery service.
“What else? Let’s see. Ah, well, not being able to offer customers as wide a selection as the big chain
bookstores. How can I turn this into a benefit?”
I stopped to think for a moment. “It means fewer books for people to have to look through to find what they want.
Perhaps I should actually promote the fact that we carry only the very best of the best: ‘We’ve carefully curated
our books, so you don’t have to wade through a mountain of them to find the ones you really want.’”
“That’s the idea,” encouraged Tom.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 8 --

“Wait, I’ve got more problems… Oh, well, I guess I can solve them anytime now that I know about these boxes.”
“This has been an amazing lesson. And to think that it all started with a thief!”
“Ah,” Tom responded dramatically, “that brings me to another of the great secrets of problem solving and
creativity: The best, most original ideas often come from outside your field of expertise.
“That’s because the ideas that come from our own fields are often polluted with inbred ways of looking at things
and false notions of what will and what won’t work. Instead, the trick is to look inside boxes from other fields and
other industries—from people who don’t think and look at the world the same way you do.
“That’s why so many breakthrough inventions were made by ‘dabblers’—people who tackled and solved a so-
called impossible problem in a field not their own, because they brought a fresh perspective.
“Alexander Graham Bell was a teacher of the deaf who dabbled in electronics when he invented the telephone.
The Wright brothers were bicycle makers who had no business dabbling in the science of aerodynamics.
“And as a very young man, Philo Farnsworth came up with the idea that made television possible as he mowed
hay on a farm in neat rows and realized that an electron beam could scan an image in horizontal lines.
“So, here’s your homework for next time, to give yourself an outsiders’ perspective: First, I want you to think of
two numbers between one and four.”
Rather puzzled, I jutted my lip and replied, “Two and four?”
“Good. Next, I want you to go to the magazine rack in your bookstore. When you get there, go to the second row,
and take the fourth magazine from the right. If it’s Publishers’ Weekly , put it back and try again until you find
something in a field totally unrelated to any of your interests. Hopefully, it will be something like Doll Collecting
Monthly . Read it and see what you learn that you can apply to your own business.
“In fact, why don’t we meet next time at your bookstore? Then, I can see how your operations work in real time,
and you can tell me all about what you’ve learned.”
Summary & Exercises
In a Nutshell
This Adventure introduces the Before and After boxes.
The questions in these boxes encourage you to think about what you can do to a thing or situation before —or
afterward —that will enable you to solve or avoid a problem, take advantage of an opportunity or do whatever
else it is that you’re trying to accomplish.
Very often, we don’t see a problem, situation or opportunity fully laid out in time. Instead, we tend to focus on only
one point in time.
Let’s say, for example, that the widgets are coming off the assembly line in a different shade of green than
they’re supposed to be. Our first thought may be: “How do we fix the assembly line, so this doesn’t happen?”
But we don’t look back in time to see if perhaps the problem is the type of widget paint we’re buying. Or to see if
the widgets would hold the color better if they were pre- coated with something else first.
And we may not look ahead in time to see whether we could promote different colors as a benefit. (“Now
available in a wide range of colors!”)
In advertising and marketing, we try to persuade customers at the point of sale, whether in a store or on the
page––and often miss what can be done that might pre-persuade: to make the prospect more receptive to our
message before they even hear it.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 8 --

In marketing, Scott Galloway suggests intentionally thinking about pre-purchase (advertising, promotion),
purchase (where and how they buy) and post-purchase (taking care of them afterwards). Apple, he points out,
does such a good job of the purchase step (those beautiful Apple stores) and taking care of the customer post-
purchase, that they need to do comparatively pre-purchase advertising.
Before we apply the Before and After boxes to your problem, I want to mention a major idea that was introduced
in this Adventure: The importance of getting (or cultivating) a fresh perspective on a problem, often with insights
from another field or industry––instead of limiting your thinking to your own particular area of expertise.
There’s a story of an old hotel that didn’t have any elevators. The hotel was losing business because people
don’t like having to cart their heavy luggage up multiple flights of stairs to get to their rooms. So, the owners
hired an engineering company to figure out where they could possibly install an elevator. The engineers were
racking their brains––while the owners were dreading the disruption the renovation would cause, not to mention
the loss of space on every floor.
Finally, a janitor—an outsider to the world of engineering whose thinking wasn’t limited by preconceived notions
of what and where an elevator had to be—suggested putting the elevator on the outside of the hotel.
The hotel owners laughed but the engineers said, “Yeah, we could do that.”
And voilà… the world’s first exterior passenger elevator was installed at the El Cortez Hotel in San Diego.
How many days did the hotel have to be closed due to construction? Zero!
Now, let’s go through the two Before and After boxes together:
Click here to download this fillable PDF
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 8 --

Click here to download this fillable PDF
Your Action Steps
1. Spend a minute right now thinking about how a naïve outsider would look at your business (or the problem or
opportunity that’s on your mind). What might they have to say about your situation? What ideas might they come
up with?
MODULES
2. Try to understand what happened to people, situations, problems, opportunities or ideas before this point in
time. Try to envision what will happen to them afterward. What might you do beforehand—or afterward—to
influence a certain outcome in your favor? Practice before and after awareness. For example, look at a coffee
cup. See it as being manufactured, Shipped to the store. Bought. See where it will end up—in the garbage?
Recycling? Lost somewhere.
It’s time for you to read Adventure #5: Lights… Camera… Change the Action.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 8 --

It’s a fun one and there’s a particularly powerful group of boxes in that Adventure.
Don’t be afraid to be funny or absurd—it may lead you someplace brilliant.
Try it out (build a prototype).
All of these resources are also available in your Idea Power Mindbook if you prefer to write
them out by hand.
